speaker: interviewer
text: --our interview with Clara Skaletzky on January 24, 1995. Would you tell us about West Germany? Now, you were able to stay in the French Zone, and then you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: To American zone [NON-ENGLISH] stay-- we were staying there, oh, for a couple of months. And then we left for West Germany, in a town. The name of the town was Eschwege-- by Cosel-- Eschwege by Cosel. And there was-- over there what they had-- they made a DP camp there. That was-- before it was a German military station. So they come by. They made it-- no Germans-- so they made it for DP camp. It was buildings and smaller buildings, larger buildings. 4,000 refugees were there-- Polish-- Russia-- no-- Russia not-- Polish, mostly Polish Jews that ran away from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day it was-- we were staying there four years. My son, older son, Harry, was born there in 1948, March 1948. I got married to the-- legally to the-- before for Jewish. And this was legally to-- my husband to says, do you want to have a-- you're gonna have a un-legitimate child?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I said, let's go to marry. A month before, two months before he got-- was born, we went to the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the legal office from the-- from-- from the German, the town over there, [NON-ENGLISH]. They called it, [NON-ENGLISH]. And we got married legally to the government-- through the German government. And then he was born with his name already-- a Skaletzky-- not Bren. [LAUGHS] And staying there with the-- with a child, and it was-- my husband had a uncle-- had a uncle in the United State. And he applied. He wants to go to the uncle. That's the only thing we had-- an unknown uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they send out some papers, but they didn't send it out-- the right-- without a child. And then we had to wait. That's why it took us about four years to come here.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister went about a year before us-- left to-- to the United State and came to Chicago. She came in-- 19-- I came in here on 1949. She came in '48. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And staying there and waiting there-- we went-- they liquidated already. It was no Jews already. Some of them left to Israel, some to another countries. So they liquidated already the DP camp there, Eschwege. So they moved us in a different city-- to Cosel. And we was waiting there. And then finally, they approved us. And we-- we went to Bremen. Bremen is the-- that's the port from Germany with ship. And the sign that's went through was all formalities. And we went through to-- to the-- to the dock. That's when-- and they let us put-- put us on a ship. The name of the ship was General Muir. And that was not a luxury ship. It was a very primitive-- a military ship. And I was with the little-- with my little child who was only eight months old. And they give me a cabin together with another two or three women-- I think two. And then there was a-- a bunk bed-- up and down. So I was upstairs. There's total-- another two-- three single women was with me in the same-- in the same cabin. And my little boy was a little baby. And I-- and he didn't want to sleep in the crib. It was a-- a little crib-- no. He wants to sleep only with me. He was afraid. So I kept him with me upstairs. And it took us 10, 12 days. I was sick like a dog on door-- on the-- on the ship-- terrible. You know, some people cannot ride. I was sick, and finally, we came back in the harbor to New York. And I was so hungry, like a-- I eat up. I saw the hot dogs with the-- with the-- with the-- the hot dog buns, you know, with the hot dogs. Oh! One, two-- I didn't ask if it's kosher or not kosher. I eat them. I was hungry! Oh, then-- and then later on, we-- the Jewish-- the Jewish agency took us with the-- with a car. Came in, a woman, a volunteer, I think, from the Jewish Federation, and took us to the train, and put us on the train to Chicago. Because we was assigned to Chicago. They came and they went. We-- all night it was traveling. It took us overnight. And I came-- we came to Chicago. And I was there with my mother-- came up and, you know, cried the river with my sister and this uncle who sent. And they have-- they had already apartment for us. In-- in Chicago there was the West Side. So we went there. We-- we start accomplishing a life, beginning-- with a child. And my husband start-- had a job. At first he got it-- a smaller job, then he got more money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then later on, he cut his finger. He told you that already. I don't have to tell you the same thing? So later-- and finally, after five years, he says he wants to go himself in business. He went in business. We managed. We made a living here. We are happy. We love America. God bless America. That's the best country from the world-- the best. You can't-- we have some problems here, too, but that can-- what can you do? Everybody, each country is worse on. This is the best.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now we are already retired people. It came up. We finished up in Chicago. And lived 8-- 38 years I lived in Chicago-- working. I worked without my husband all my life. I had a-- I was-- like my mother told me, you have to be 50/50 with your husband. All my life, I helped him in the business. And all over, I was 50/50 with him. And here, right now, the same thing. I-- I am-- we came up here in the-- to California. The reason what we came is for the climate. I liked Chicago very much, but the climate is too rough on us. We getting older.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came up here, and we established himself here. We live here. We like it here. It's a little bit problems, but we like it. That's what that is. What else will you want to know about the States? What happened to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was killed in 1942 in-- in September. They-- they, together he was hiding with my husband's father, and my uncle, and aunt, in a hiding place in the ghetto. And they find him there. And they take-- took him to the Jewish cemetery, and they killed him. That's all what I know about him. I don't-- I never saw his grave. I don't know where his-- where his bones are. That's what people told us-- all of them are-- all four people in the same one grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you learn that? He-- he said to your mother, save the children. When was that? And then you left. And when did you think he-- explain the time difference between his telling your mother that, your leaving, and then what you learned and when about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we left the ghetto-- run away, from that time till-- till the liberation, when I came to Korets in May, '44, we didn't know where he-- what's-- what's happened to him. He came into Korets. My husband-- my future husband told me there that he talked with Ukrainian people. And the Ukrainian people told him that they-- they saw them. They took them out from the-- from the hiding place, and they killed them on the cemetery. That's all what I know-- not more.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to them, what they did, how they find them-- I don't know. I was in a different place, and they was, you know, in-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect your mother, and you, and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the whole thing? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: The loss of your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very badly. In fact, I was emotional before talking about my father. I'm emotional, because I don't know what-- at least my mother died here. I know the grave. I go there, and I visit her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, I don't know nothing-- where his bones are, what happened to him, how did he die? It affect us very, very badly. The morning, if it was morning all the time, all over in the hiding place, I knew-- we knew that he was-- I-- I even had a dream one time. I saw my father that he came in. And he told me, you know what? They killed me. They just put a bullet in the neck of-- in the back of my neck. And I was killed-- a dream I had like that. That's all what I know-- nothing else. At what point did you learn exactly about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: What point? What do mean a point?

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when-- when was it you learned about him? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came in-- in 1940? In 19-- I knew that he's-- he was killed. But I didn't know when. But when we came in back to the town after the liberation, I find it out. This was in '44-- in May '44, when I came to the town-- find it out that it was-- they killed him. They killed four people and put them in-- put them up to the-- in the cemetery, and they killed them. They did to all of them, whatever they caught at that time-- alive-- hiding, you know, going around someplace. They brought them to the cemetery, and they killed them. That's what the-- the Ukrainian people told us. That's all what they told us. Then, that's all what I know-- nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about the time when you were separated from your father? You and your mother and your sister, you-- you split off-- what-- what he did different than-- and you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was left with his sister, a sister and brother-in-law. He says, go. And I cannot-- I'm a-- I look Jewish, very Jewish. I'm not going to be able to go through even. Maybe you gonna go through with the kids. Go and save the kids. So my father sacrificed his life-- sacrificed his life. That's the way we parted-- we parted. I took a few pictures of myself from when I was just a little girl, and a small girl, and then my father and my mother. And I put it in like a passport pictures. And I put this in pocket. Actually, they have a little thing to-- to-- to see-- have on it. That's all what I had. I had-- I had that mind to take some pictures. And I have them till now. That's it. That's over. That's the way the-- we parted. At that time, you didn't-- you didn't have time to-- to do something. You-- you ran. You-- you're young. You-- you are [NON-ENGLISH]. You want to live-- that the-- the-- the power for living is so high. Every person wants to live-- especially in a-- in a situation like we was.

speaker: interviewee
text: They say, even if you-- the-- if you are drowning, you're drinking. You know drowning? You go in the river, you-- you-- even a little straw, you try to-- to pull yourself up-- a little thing. You want to pull yourself up to-- to stay alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody wants to do this thing. Every human being wants to live. Even if you are 100 years old, you're afraid of death. Everybody's afraid. I don't know, maybe it's bad and maybe it's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And your father's sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dead-- all of them dead-- died. I had some sisters in a different town. The whole family is perished. That there is not one left from the family. My-- my-- my father's older sister-- not one left-- everything perished. And my mother's sisters all perished-- cousins-- small town, not far from our town. Everything is gone-- nothing. Six million Jews-- it's a-- it's a joke. It's not a joke-- one million children, five million adults was perished in that second World War. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's left? A little-- very little, maybe-- I don't know, 1/2 a million of us. Most of them went to Israel-- a few to the states, a few to Canada. This is only younger people. Older-- nothing left from older. That's the younger. My age is already the youngest.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was younger, and that's still young. Some of them are already dead-- the survivors-- older. They're not alive. That's the-- that's the end already-- the last survivors. We are the last ones. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now it's 50 years--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --almost to the day. What message or lesson would you like to give for your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Future--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your children, or to--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for my children?

speaker: interviewer
text: --the grandchildren, the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: A message-- never again. Don't let yourself down. And add all the time up for a better tomorrow-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --for everybody. We'll look at pictures in a moment. I want to ask you about Chicago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked when you first came to Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I did work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A dressmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me what you did when you got to Chicago? You had one child.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one child, and my mother was still alive. And she was babysitting with my child. And I tried to help out still my husband, make a few-- the more wages I went and saw a dressmaker in a-- in a dress shop, and I worked as a-- a seamstress. I worked till I got pregnant with the second son, with this one. When I was already pregnant, I-- I couldn't with two children. It was very hard. I stopped doing that. But a year I worked in a dress shop in Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my-- I-- and I gave birth to my son. And I was staying home. I didn't do nothing. I had to raise the kids, the children, after five years. My kids grow-- and went-- we-- you know, growed up a little, by us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sent them to-- to school. They went to school-- went up to school. And I used to-- whenever they was in school, I went to help my-- my husband in the-- in the business. I helped him out practically about three, four times a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fridays I used to stay home and make for-- usually traditional-- every time Friday I used to make the traditional Shabbat dinner. I never cooked the Shabbat. I worked on Shabbat, but never cooked. I cooked up Friday, and we had Shabbas. We eat-- cooked meal-- fresh-- you know, ready meal. That's the way I feel. And this, today too. Here I don't do shopping for-- Saturday, I don't do shopping. My husband wants to go shopping. I said, no-- not Saturday. I have six days. I can-- I can do on six days. On Saturday, I rest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's-- that's from the beginning, the-- they putting-- they giving you-- your parents gives you a start like that. You have to-- you have to be like-- you have to conduct yourself like that. That's the way I am now. I have a kosher home. I kept kosher in my house. I only had-- everything kosher. Since I came to America, never used something else, even outside when I eat-- unless in a fish. I eat fish outside. That's all. I don't eat non-kosher foods-- everything kosher. I don't know. Maybe it's wrong. To me, it's OK. It's the right way to do it. Maybe people are laughing on me. I don't care. That's-- I'm in a free country, I can do what I want it. I want to have kosher, I have kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Let's look at some pictures now. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is Clara Skaletzky's father. His name was Hirsch Bren And he was born in 1889, killed in 1942 by the Germans-- Nazis. It was-- this picture's made from a passport in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: More about my father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Owns a bodega-- he was a businessman, in grocery business-- all his life was working. And for the Germans, he was doing labor work. He worked very hard. And during the-- the Russian occupation, he was-- he worked-- because he was a businessman, he worked as a janitor in a office. And through my father, I-- the-- the-- the main man from the office got me to a school, a higher school in a bigger town, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was Rovno And I was there, educated, and went to school about a year until the German occupied the Ukraine. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the ghetto was liquidated and my-- we parted with my father-- so I had in my mind, I took a-- I took a couple of pictures in my pocket. And they were small pictures from-- passport pictures. I put them in my pocket, and that's the way I had the pictures. And this picture is enlarged. And this picture was made, I think, in 1938 for a passport, a Polish passport. And it-- and it was made in Korets-- in Korets. And that was my-- and this is my father. Mine-- my father's name is Hirschel Bren. My name, maiden name, is Bren too. And we parted. And he was killed by the German Nazis in 1942. I don't know even where his grave is located, because we left the ghetto and never saw each other again. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my mother, Clara Skaletzky's mother. Her name is Bella, maiden name Barg, and the marriage name, Bren. The spelling is B-R-E-N. Maiden name spelling is Barg, B-A-R-G, 'Berg'. She was born in 1887-- very nice woman. I loved her very much. She saved-- she-- she saved my life-- took-- took us to the village where she was born-- and the name of the village, Rilovka. And she tried. And she hided us from the Nazis between her neighbors-- best neighbors. They helped. They helped us to stay alive for a year and a 1/2. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [BACKGROUND NOISE] The picture was taken in the United States-- I think-- in Germany. I don't-- excuse me. The picture was taken in Germany, in Eschwege, in 1948. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They came here-- she came to the States. She lived here two years and became sick. And she died here. And I know where she-- she died in Chicago, and I have the grave. And I visit the grave practically every couple years. That's-- that's all. That's everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Start? This is the family what-- they saved my life. Six months I was hiding by them. This is Mr. and Mrs. Storazuk. And this is their daughter, Nadia Storazuk. Actually, she's-- she was the one what saved us from the German catching us in the-- in the village. She came into the house, pushed us out from the house and told us to run in the woods. And we runned away. And then 10 minutes later, the German was running and looking for us. And couldn't-- and they didn't' find us. So I give respect to this girl, Nadia Storazuk. She saved my life, my mother, and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Date-- photo-- the-- this photo is made, I think, before World War II. Practically, I cannot say exactly. I imagine maybe it's in 1935, '35, '36. The date? No, I don't know. Place? The photo was made in Rilovka, a-- the place, this village where I was saved. And my mother was born there. That's it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture, and far left, the girl what saved my life. The previous picture was-- was far right. The name of the girl is Nadia Storazuk. And the place is Israel, here. We took her to Israel for a trip in 19-- what was the year? 19-- 1992, 1992 in-- in May. And we made it for a-- for a gentle, righteous woman. And she's a-- she had a plaque in Yad Vashem with her name, with the name of her parents, that they were righteous gentiles. And this is the girl what saved our life. And I was hiding there for six months by her parents. And when they have to-- they start-- the Germans start to take them for work to Germany, they was afraid they'll catch me there. And they'll-- and they'll kill them all out. So I had to leave and go to a different place. But they was righteous people-- the very nice, gentle people. God bless them. Oh. They're still alive. This is still alive. That's me and that's our guide, American born man what he took us there. And he guided us through Israel. I was with her 10 days and shared that wonderful time. And I left her a lot of presents and gifts. And she took it. She-- and she took the-- and she took it with her to-- to Russia. She has children, and she's going to share it with her children, all the things what I give her. That's it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Nadia Storazuk is 71 years old. And she lives now still in the village of Rilovka. And she owns a little farm. And she's taking care on her-- herself. She's a widow. She doesn't have a husband. She lost her husband in a accident. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK? All right? This is the daughter from Ivan Chaichuk-- what he first-- first saved us from the Germans. He give-- he came out and greet my mother and gived her food in the woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: About a couple times-- a couple of weeks-- a couple of weeks in the evening, we used to meet each other in the woods. And he brought us-- his-- her father brought us the food. And he-- he guided us. And he give me a place where to stay later on. And this is his granddaughter on the right. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- the daughter, our main-- the daughter of Mr.-- Mr. Chaichuk-- Ivan Chaichuk. Her name is Mariia-- Mariia. She was-- at that time, I wasn't-- in that time, when I was in the hiding place, she was about 4 years old. And she knew already that I have to be-- I couldn't go out on the outside. So she-- she saw somebody, a little girl, and she says, Tanya, Tanya! Go hide yourself. People are coming here. That's what I remember her-- very nice little girl. I used to play with her on the-- on the attic. She used to come up, and I played with her, games. Didn't have nothing to do, so I had to play with the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and you had a name-- they gave you a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tanya-- I told Tanya. My name was Katanya in the hiding place. They called me Tanya. My-- my real name is Clara Skaletzky. And the hiding place, was Tanya-- not-- not last name, just one name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and will you tell us who's left and who's right once again, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left is Mariia, this daughter of Ivan Chaichuk. And right is Maria's daughter. That's a granddaughter of Ivan Chaichuk.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in Rilovka, around 1980-- 1980, I think. Yeah.
